activity_metadata.objective: Students will analyze how text structure develops the central idea in "Everybody Jump."
activity_metadata.alignment_long: This activity replaces the "Read" section of the Close Read lesson for "Everybody Jump (from 'What If?')." It offers a scaffolded sequence that guides students through analyzing how Munroe uses informational text structures—including cause-and-effect relationships, sequential organization, and contrasting sections—to develop the central idea that humans have a much greater impact on each other than they do on the planet.
activity_metadata.connection_to_end_of_unit_assessment_tas: This activity supports the end-of-unit Informative Writing Project by developing students' ability to analyze how authors organize information to develop central ideas. By analyzing Munroe's structural choices—such as using cause-and-effect to show cascading failures or sequential order to emphasize collapse speed—students learn how organizational patterns emphasize key ideas. These principles will help students structure their own informative essays with clear thesis statements and supporting details.
activity_metadata.unit_level_connection: This activity connects to the essential question "How do we know what to do when there are no instructions?" by examining what happens when human systems break down. Munroe's thought experiment shows that humans rely on interconnected systems (cell networks, transportation, food distribution) that function as "instructions" for modern life. When these systems disappear in Rhode Island, chaos ensues. The cascading cause-and-effect structure emphasizes how quickly society collapses without the infrastructure that guides daily decisions, directly addressing what happens when instructions are absent.
activity_metadata.excerpt:
Everybody Jump (from 'What If?')

(1) What would happen if everyone on earth stood as close to each other as they could and jumped, everyone landing on the ground at the same instant? —Thomas Bennett (and many others)

(2) This is one of the most popular questions submitted to this blog. It’s been examined before, including by a ScienceBlogs post and a Straight Dope article. They cover the kinematics pretty well. However, they don’t tell the whole story.

(3) Let’s take a closer look.

(4) At the start of the scenario, the entire Earth’s population has been magically transported together into one place.

(5) This crowd takes up an area the size of Rhode Island. But there’s no reason to use the vague phrase “an area the size of Rhode Island”. This is our scenario; we can be specific. They’re actually in Rhode Island.

(6) At the stroke of noon, everyone jumps.

(7) As discussed elsewhere, it doesn’t really affect the planet. Earth outweighs us by a factor of over ten trillion. On average, we humans can vertically jump maybe half a meter on a good day. Even if the Earth were rigid and responded instantly, it would be pushed down by less than an atom’s width.

(8) Next, everyone falls back to the ground.

(9) Technically, this delivers a lot of energy into the Earth, but it’s spread out over a large enough area that it doesn’t do much more than leave footprints in a lot of gardens. A slight pulse of pressure spreads through the North American continental crust and dissipates with little effect. The sound of all those feet hitting the ground creates a loud, drawn-out roar which lasts many seconds.

(10) Eventually, the air grows quiet.

(11) Seconds pass. Everyone looks around.

(12) There are a lot of uncomfortable glances. Someone coughs.

(13) A cell phone comes out of a pocket. Within seconds, the rest of the world’s five billion phones follow. All of them—even those compatible with the region’s towers—are displaying some version of “NO SIGNAL”. The cell networks have all collapsed under the unprecedented load.

(14) The T. F. Green airport in Warwick, Rhode Island handles a few thousand passengers a day. Assuming they got things organized (including sending out scouting missions to retrieve fuel), they could run at 500% capacity for years without making a dent in the crowd.

(15) The addition of all the nearby airports doesn’t change the equation much. Nor does the region’s light rail system. Crowds climb on board container ships in the deepwater port of Providence, but stocking sufficient food and water for a long sea voyage proves a challenge.

(16) Rhode Island’s half-million cars are commandeered. Moments later, I-95, I-195, and I-295 become the sites of the largest traffic jam in the history of the planet. Most of the cars are engulfed by the crowds, but a lucky few get out and begin wandering the abandoned road network.

(17) Some make it past New York or Boston before running out of fuel. Since the electricity is probably not on at this point, rather than find a working gas pump, it’s easier to just abandon the car and steal the new one. Who can stop you? All the cops are in Rhode Island.

(18) The edge of the crowd spreads outward into southern Massachusetts and Connecticut. Any two people who meet are unlikely to have a language in common, and almost nobody knows the area. The state becomes a patchwork chaos of coalescing and collapsing social hierarchies. Violence is common. Everybody is hungry and thirsty. Grocery stores are emptied. Fresh water is hard to come by and there’s no efficient system for distributing it.

(19) Within weeks, Rhode Island is a graveyard of billions.

(20) The survivors spread out across the face of the world and struggle to build a new civilization atop the pristine ruins of the old. Our species staggers on, but our population has been greatly reduced. Earth’s orbit is completely unaffected—it spins along exactly as it did before our species-wide jump.

(21) But at least now we know.
exit_ticket:
The central idea of “Everybody Jump” is that humans affect each other more than they affect the planet. How does Munroe's use of text structure help develop this central idea? 


Write a one-sentence claim identifying a specific structural choice and explaining its purpose.

Respond in the form of a paragraph that includes a claim, two pieces of evidence, and an explanation.

USER_14.question: In paragraphs 7-9, Munroe analyzes what happens to Earth. How does he organize this analysis, and why might he organize it this way?

responses.content: A. Description - organizing by describing what things look like or what happens
C. Cause and effect - showing how one event leads to another event

USER_14.question: Highlight one sentence or phrase from paragraphs 13-17 that shows a cause-and-effect relationship.

responses.content: He organizes it by showing the problem, no solution, and shows what the problem does to the rest of the people. He might do this to show the reader how one thing can lead to another.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response describes the organization as 'showing the problem, no solution, and shows what the problem does to the rest of the people,' which is a problem-solution structure. This contradicts the actual structure in the text, which is chronological/sequential (first the jump, then the landing, then the effects). The response does attempt to give a reason for the organization, but the misidentification of the structure is a disqualifier, making this 'incorrect.'

responses.content: It talks about how  the jump won’t do anything to the planet. Its a cause and effect organized text.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response identifies that the text is organized by cause and effect and mentions that the jump doesn't do anything to the planet, which shows some engagement with the structure. However, it does not clearly identify the chronological or sequential structure (first jump, then fall, then aftermath) or provide a clear reason for why Munroe organizes it this way. The answer does not contradict the text but is missing one part of the required answer, so it fits best in the 'partial-understanding' bucket.

responses.content: First, people jump, the earth barely takes any effect, then people fall down back to the ground, lastly it makes a loud drawn-out sound.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response correctly identifies the sequence of events (first, people jump; then, the Earth barely takes any effect; then, people fall back; lastly, it makes a loud sound), which shows understanding of the chronological/step-by-step structure. However, the response does not clearly explain WHY Munroe organizes it this way (the purpose or effect of using this structure). Since only the structure is addressed and not the purpose, this fits the 'partial-understanding' bucket.

USER_14.question: Look at paragraph 18. What happens as a result of people not having a common language and not knowing the area?

responses.content: A cell phone comes out of a pocket. Within seconds, the rest of the world’s five billion phones follow. All of them—even those compatible with the region’s towers—are displaying some version of “NO SIGNAL”
responses.bucket_rationale: The student's highlight is: "A cell phone comes out of a pocket. Within seconds, the rest of the world’s five billion phones follow. All of them—even those compatible with the region’s towers—are displaying some version of “NO SIGNAL”". The longest acceptable answer is 232 characters. Calculating the character limit: 232 x 1.3 = 301.6, 232 + 50 = 282. The limit is 302 characters. The student's highlight is 217 characters, which is within the limit. The highlighted text also directly shows a cause-and-effect relationship: the action of everyone pulling out their phones causes the cell networks to collapse. Therefore, the response is correct.

USER_14.question: What is the central idea of “Everybody Jump”?

responses.content: No one can communicate and because of that, there is a lot of violence, and no one can peacefully pass by.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response identifies that people cannot communicate, which is a direct result of not having a common language, and links this to violence and the inability to peacefully pass by. This matches the key consequences described in paragraph 18: violence and social breakdown due to language barriers and unfamiliarity. The answer is specific, text-engaged, and aligns with the meaning of the paragraph.

USER_14.question:
The central idea of “Everybody Jump” is that humans affect each other more than they affect the planet. How does Munroe's use of text structure help develop this central idea? 


Write a one-sentence claim identifying a specific structural choice and explaining its purpose.

responses.content: People take more impact on each other than the earth.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response restates the central idea but does not identify any structural choice or explain how the structure develops the central idea. It only discusses the theme, not the text structure, which is required by the prompt.

responses.content: People take more impact on each other than the earth. Because society is everyone as a whole, so if a nation is mad at another, it may start the third world war due to alliances and rivals.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response restates the central idea but does not identify any structural choice from the text. It discusses how people affect each other and gives an example about nations and war, which is not related to the structure of the passage or how Munroe uses structure to develop the central idea. There is no mention of contrast, cause-and-effect, chronological order, or any other structural technique. Therefore, it fits the 'incorrect' bucket.

responses.content: People take more impact on each other than the earth. Because society is everyone as a whole, so if a nation is mad at another, it may start the third world war due to alliances and rivals. Munroe uses cause and effect to organize this text.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response identifies a structural choice (cause and effect) and mentions the central idea (people affect each other more than the Earth), but the explanation of how the structure develops the central idea is vague and not directly connected to the text's organization. The sentence is complete, but the connection between structure and purpose is not clearly explained, which fits the 'partial-understanding' bucket.

USER_14.question:
The central idea of “Everybody Jump” is that humans affect each other more than they affect the planet. How does Munroe's use of text structure help develop this central idea? Choose one specific way Munroe structures the text and explain how it develops the central idea.


Respond in the form of a paragraph that includes a claim, two pieces of evidence, and an explanation.

responses.content: Munroe uses cause and effect to show humans have a larger impact on each other than they do on the planet. Munroe shows this by stating if 1 person pulls out their phone, others may get the same idea leading to everyone who has a phone using it, causing the networks to collapse due to the overload, same thing if someone steals, if one person does it, everyone will. Causing every society to collapse.
responses.bucket_rationale: The response identifies a structural choice (cause and effect), provides two pieces of evidence (the phone network collapse and the spread of stealing leading to societal collapse), and explains how these effects show humans impact each other more than the planet. The explanation connects the evidence to the claim by showing how one person's actions lead to widespread consequences among people, which supports the central idea. The language and reasoning are appropriate for a fourth-grade level, and the response meets all requirements for a score of 3.
